mechanism_explained: Roadmap explains programming as conducting and the module progression.
The chapter turns AI use from hidden assistance into inspectable process evidence.
The chapter makes the solve/verify asymmetry operational through problem formulation and binary handoff conditions.
The chapter uses Java signatures and invariants as the first concrete language of specification.
The chapter deep-dives how object boundaries enforce invariants across an object's lifecycle.
The chapter makes system-scale specification visible through problem summaries and dependency-ordered handoffs.
The chapter uses LSP audits to turn inheritance from taxonomy into behavioral verification.
The chapter translates user needs into JavaFX controls, event sources, and handler contracts.
The chapter forces local specifications to survive system-level integration and peer challenge.
The chapter separates signature compatibility from behavioral interchangeability.
The chapter turns event handlers into auditable state transitions with guards and no-op cases.
The chapter connects Java type safety and collection choice to explicit access-pattern requirements.
The chapter teaches recursive methods as auditable decomposition contracts with termination evidence.
The chapter elevates collection and storage choices into explicit architecture decisions tied to invariants and load.
The chapter teaches failure-state enumeration, risk triage, and prompt revision without scope expansion.
The chapter integrates the whole course into architectural decision defense and cascade-risk accountability.
Appendix consolidates the course's recurring supervisory themes.
